_note: PLACEHOLDER DATA for demo purposes. Replace with veterinary-verified content before any real-world use. Sources should be added (e.g. Ghana Ministry of Food and Agriculture, FAO poultry health guides) before production deployment.

diseases.name: Newcastle Disease
clip_labels: a sick chicken with twisted neck and breathing problems
a chicken with newcastle disease symptoms
treatment_steps: Isolate visibly sick birds from the rest of the flock immediately.
There is no cure once symptoms appear — focus on supportive care: warmth, clean water, electrolytes.
Vaccinate the remaining healthy flock immediately (e.g. with I-2 or La Sota strain vaccine) to limit spread.
Disinfect housing, feeders, and waterers with a household disinfectant (e.g. diluted bleach 1:10).
Contact a local veterinary officer or agricultural extension agent to confirm diagnosis and report the outbreak.

quarantine.reason: Newcastle Disease is highly contagious and spreads rapidly through respiratory droplets, contaminated feed/water, and equipment. It can wipe out an entire flock within days.

diseases.name: Fowl Pox
clip_labels: a chicken with wart-like skin lesions on its comb and wattle
a chicken with fowl pox lesions
symptoms: wart-like nodules on comb, wattle, and skin
lesions in the mouth and throat (wet form)
treatment_steps: Isolate affected birds to reduce spread, though fowl pox spreads relatively slowly.
Apply a mild antiseptic (e.g. diluted iodine) to skin lesions to prevent secondary bacterial infection.
Ensure good nutrition to support the bird's immune system during recovery (usually 3-4 weeks).
Vaccinate unaffected birds in the flock if pox is confirmed, as there is no treatment for the virus itself.
Control mosquitoes and biting insects around the coop, as they are the main carriers.

quarantine.reason: Fowl pox spreads through mosquito bites and direct contact with lesions or contaminated surfaces; isolating sick birds reduces transmission risk while the rest of the flock is vaccinated.

recovery_feed_additions: high-protein feed to support skin healing

diseases.name: Coccidiosis
clip_labels: a chicken with bloody diarrhea and ruffled feathers
a lethargic chicken with bloody droppings
treatment_steps: Move birds to a dry, clean area — coccidiosis thrives in damp, dirty litter.
Administer an anticoccidial medication (e.g. amprolium) in drinking water, following the dosage on the product label.
Provide clean water with electrolytes to prevent dehydration.
Remove wet litter and replace with dry bedding immediately.
Consult a local agrovet for the correct anticoccidial product and dosage for your flock size.

quarantine.reason: Coccidiosis is caused by parasites already present in the environment rather than direct bird-to-bird contagion in the way viral diseases spread; the priority is treating affected birds and cleaning the environment rather than isolating them.

diseases.name: No Disease Detected
clip_labels: a healthy active chicken with bright eyes and smooth feathers
treatment_steps: No treatment needed. Continue routine flock monitoring and good biosecurity practices.
